name: Information Systems Management

sessions.title: IS Introduction
topics: Information systems fundamentals and management concepts

sessions.title: ERP Systems
topics: Enterprise Resource Planning fundamentals, architecture, benefits

sessions.title: ERP Key Modules
topics: Finance, HR, Supply Chain, Manufacturing modules and integration

sessions.title: IS Life Cycle
topics: Project management, implementation phases, change management

sessions.title: Feasibility Study
topics: Technical, economic, organizational, schedule feasibility assessment

topics: ROI, NPV, payback period, and other financial metrics for IS investments

definitions.definition: Integrated set of components that collect, process, store, and deliver information to support decision-making and organizational operations
Integrated software system managing business processes and data across entire organization; unifies finance, HR, supply chain, manufacturing, and sales
Process of connecting different IT systems and applications to work together seamlessly and share data
Comprehensive analysis evaluating technical, economic, organizational, and scheduling feasibility of proposed IS project
Process of managing organizational and human aspects of system implementation to ensure adoption and success
Framework of policies, procedures, and decision-making authority for managing IS projects
Financial metric measuring benefits generated by IS investment relative to costs; calculated as (Benefits - Costs) / Costs

concepts.name: IS Life Cycle
concepts.explanation: Phases of IS project: planning, requirements analysis, design, development/selection, implementation, operation, maintenance. Each phase has specific objectives, activities, and deliverables

concepts.name: ERP Implementation
concepts.explanation: Complex organizational change involving technology selection, business process redesign, data migration, staff training, and change management to integrate systems

concepts.name: Systems Development Approaches
concepts.explanation: Different methodologies: Waterfall (sequential phases), Agile (iterative, responsive), Hybrid. Choice depends on project complexity, requirements stability, and organizational context

concepts.name: IS Investment Evaluation
concepts.explanation: Financial and non-financial assessment of IS projects using metrics like NPV, ROI, payback period, and qualitative benefits (competitive advantage, risk reduction, capability building)

concepts.name: Enterprise Architecture
concepts.explanation: Blueprint for organizing business processes and IT systems to support organizational strategy; ensures alignment, integration, and scalability

concepts.name: Vendor Evaluation & Selection
concepts.explanation: Process of identifying, evaluating, and selecting software vendors and implementation partners based on functional fit, cost, support, implementation capability

distinctions.difference: ERP: company owns and manages system, controls processes; Outsourcing: external partner manages processes and systems
Technical: can the system be built/configured? Does it meet specifications?; Organizational: will users adopt it? Does organization have capacity/commitment?

examples: Lenzing textile company ERP implementation case study - challenges, solutions, lessons learned
SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics ERP systems
Financial module implementation - accounting, payables, receivables
Supply chain integration through ERP systems
Change management in large-scale system implementations

questions.question: Define an Information System and describe the three main frameworks used to analyse IS within an organisation. For each framework, explain what perspective it provides.
questions.model_answer:
An Information System is an integrated set of components that collect, process, store, and distribute information to support decision-making, coordination, and control in an organisation. It combines technology (hardware, software, databases) with people and processes.

Three frameworks: (A) IS by Components — analyses the technical building blocks: hardware, software, databases, and managers/people. Shows what the system is made of. (B) IS by User Level — maps systems to the organisational hierarchy. At the operational level: Transaction Processing Systems (TPS) handle routine transactions. At middle management: Management Information Systems (MIS) and Decision Support Systems (DSS). At executive level: Executive Support Systems (ESS). Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems span all levels. (C) IS by Value Chain — maps information systems to primary activities (inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing, service) and support activities (HR, procurement, technology development, firm infrastructure). Shows how IS supports core business processes.

The three frameworks together give a complete picture: what the system contains, who uses it at what level, and what business processes it supports.
key_points: Precise definition of IS
Framework A: components (hardware, software, database, people)
Framework B: user levels (operational/TPS, middle/MIS/DSS, executive/ESS)
Mention ERP spanning levels
Framework C: value chain (primary + support activities)
Explain what each framework reveals

questions.question: What is an ERP system? Describe its key characteristics and explain the main modules typically found in an ERP implementation.
questions.model_answer:
Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) is an integrated software system that manages business processes and data across an entire organisation in a single unified system. Key characteristics: (1) Integration — all modules share a common database, eliminating data silos; (2) Real-time data — transactions update the system immediately across all functions; (3) Standardisation — ERP enforces standard business processes; (4) Scalability — systems can grow with the organisation.

Key modules: Finance and Accounting (general ledger, accounts payable/receivable, financial reporting); Human Resources (payroll, recruitment, performance management); Supply Chain Management (procurement, inventory, logistics); Manufacturing/Operations (production planning, quality management); Sales and Customer Management (order processing, CRM integration).

The Lenzing case illustrates the complexity of ERP implementation — integrating legacy systems, managing change, and achieving expected benefits requires sustained organisational commitment beyond the technical implementation.
key_points: Clear definition of ERP
Integration as core characteristic (single database)
Real-time processing
At least 4 key modules with brief description
Note implementation complexity (change management)
Optional: Lenzing reference

questions.question: Describe the main phases of the IS life cycle. What are the key activities and deliverables in each phase?
questions.model_answer: The IS life cycle describes the stages from initial concept through to operation and eventual retirement of a system. Main phases: (1) Planning — identify business need, define scope, conduct initial feasibility, obtain executive sponsorship. Deliverable: project charter. (2) Requirements Analysis — gather and document detailed functional and non-functional requirements from stakeholders. Deliverable: requirements specification. (3) Design — specify system architecture, data structures, interfaces, and processes. Deliverable: design document. (4) Development/Selection — either build custom system or select and configure a vendor solution (make vs. buy decision). Deliverable: configured/built system. (5) Testing — verify system meets requirements; includes unit testing, integration testing, user acceptance testing. (6) Implementation — deploy system, migrate data, train users, manage change. Deliverable: live system. (7) Operation and Maintenance — ongoing support, performance monitoring, updates, and enhancements. The life cycle is iterative in modern Agile approaches rather than strictly sequential as in traditional Waterfall.
key_points: Name all major phases (minimum: planning, requirements, design, build/select, implement, operate)
Brief activity description for each phase
Name key deliverables
Distinguish Waterfall vs Agile approaches
Note change management as part of implementation

questions.question: What is a feasibility study in the context of an IS project? Describe the four main types of feasibility that must be assessed.
questions.model_answer:
A feasibility study is a comprehensive preliminary analysis conducted before committing resources to an IS project. Its purpose is to determine whether the proposed system should be built and whether it can deliver the expected value. It is conducted early in the IS lifecycle, during the planning phase.

Four types of feasibility: (1) Technical Feasibility — can the system be built with available or acquirable technology? Does the organisation have or can it develop the necessary technical capabilities? (2) Economic Feasibility — will the benefits outweigh the costs? This involves estimating development costs, ongoing operational costs, and quantifying expected benefits (ROI, NPV, payback period). (3) Organisational Feasibility — does the organisation have the management commitment, human resources, and cultural readiness to implement and adopt the system? Will users accept the change? (4) Schedule Feasibility — can the system be delivered within the required timeframe? Are the time constraints realistic given scope and resources?

A negative finding on any one of these dimensions should trigger a re-evaluation of the project.
key_points: Define feasibility study and its purpose
Technical feasibility
Economic feasibility (with financial metrics)
Organisational feasibility (change management link)
Schedule feasibility
Note that all four must be positive for project to proceed

questions.question: Describe the main financial metrics used to evaluate an IS investment and explain what each metric reveals about the viability of the project.
questions.model_answer:
IS investment decisions require financial evaluation to justify expenditure and compare alternatives. Key metrics: (1) Return on Investment (ROI) — measures total benefits relative to total costs as a percentage: (Benefits - Costs) / Costs × 100. Reveals overall profitability but ignores timing of cash flows. (2) Net Present Value (NPV) — discounts future cash flows to present value, accounting for time value of money. A positive NPV means the investment creates value; negative NPV destroys value. Preferred for large, long-term investments. (3) Payback Period — how long until cumulative benefits equal the initial investment. Simpler but ignores cash flows after payback. Useful for liquidity-constrained organisations. (4) Internal Rate of Return (IRR) — the discount rate at which NPV equals zero; compared against the organisation's hurdle rate.

Critical caveat: IS investments often generate intangible benefits (improved decision-making, competitive advantage, risk reduction) that are difficult to quantify. Financial metrics capture only the quantifiable dimension; qualitative assessment of strategic benefits is also essential.
key_points: ROI definition and formula
NPV and time value of money
Payback period and its limitation
IRR concept
Acknowledge intangible benefits as limitation of purely financial analysis

questions.question:
Southern Comfort Homes Inc. (SCH) is an award-winning custom property builder who has built over 175 homes since 2000 in coastal North Carolina (USA). Their target market is residential projects for retirees looking to build their dream home. The business objective is to have a continual rolling schedule of 10–12 new construction projects each year. To attract new projects, SCH uses direct mailing to purchasers of new lots, social media, annual events, and display advertising. Landing a new project involves face-to-face meetings, design services, long-term project planning, and competitive bidding. Many projects start as prospects introduced to the company years before construction begins (even 10 years before) — leading to an extremely long sales cycle. Recently SCH selected Really Simple Systems, a cloud solution for small businesses at competitive price. Using this system has improved SCH's ability to communicate with prospects and internally, manage the day-to-day, follow up via Task Report every two weeks, and view customer history.

Given the above description, what kind of IS has SCH adopted? Can you recognise some of the main modules which typically compose this kind of IS? Upon why have they chosen a cloud solution?
questions.model_answer:
SCH has adopted a CRM (Customer Relationship Management) system. This is an operational information system designed to manage and support the entire customer lifecycle — from initial prospect contact through to project completion and post-sales relationship. Key evidence: the system tracks prospect history over many years, generates follow-up task reports, manages day-to-day communication with prospects and internally, and provides customer history views — all defining characteristics of CRM.

Main CRM modules: (1) Contact and account management — stores all prospect and client records, contact details, relationship history; (2) Sales pipeline / opportunity tracking — tracks each prospect's stage in the sales process from initial contact to signed contract, critical for SCH's 10-year sales cycles; (3) Activity and task management — generates the biweekly Task Report for follow-up, ensuring no prospect is forgotten; (4) Customer interaction history — full log of every meeting, call, email, and event, enabling personalised long-term relationships; (5) Reporting and dashboards — summary of pipeline health, upcoming follow-ups, and business performance.

SCH chose a cloud solution for three main reasons: (1) Cost — no upfront hardware or server investment; subscription pricing is affordable for a small business; (2) Accessibility — staff and management can access the system from site, meetings, or home, important for a field-based business; (3) SMB-fit — Really Simple Systems is designed for small businesses, meaning low IT overhead, fast setup, and no dedicated IT team needed. Additionally, automatic updates and data backup are handled by the vendor.
key_points: Correctly identify the IS type as CRM (Customer Relationship Management)
Evidence from the case: long sales cycle, prospect tracking, follow-up task reports, customer history
At least 4 modules: contact management, sales pipeline, task/activity management, customer history, reporting
Cloud rationale: cost (subscription vs. upfront), accessibility (remote/field access), SMB-appropriate (no IT overhead)
Optional: link CRM to broader IS taxonomy — operational IS supporting the sales primary activity in the value chain

questions.question:
During a guest speech by a digital service provider, the speaker discussed the following services provided to customers worldwide: (a) digital needs assessment and technology roadmap consulting; (b) custom software design and architecture; (c) software development, configuration, and system integration; (d) quality assurance testing and user acceptance testing; (e) system deployment, data migration, and end-user training; (f) ongoing technical support, maintenance, and system updates.

After having introduced the concept of the IS life cycle, imagine in which steps the above services can be required by customers and make some examples to clarify your points.
questions.model_answer:
The IS life cycle describes the sequential phases an information system goes through from initial idea to retirement. The digital service provider's offerings map to these phases as follows:

(1) Planning phase — Service (a): digital needs assessment and technology roadmap. The provider helps the client define the business problem, assess feasibility (technical, economic, organisational, schedule), set scope, and create a roadmap before any investment is committed.

(2) Requirements analysis phase — Service (a) extends here: translating business needs into specific functional and non-functional requirements. Example: for a retail company implementing an ERP, the provider documents which transactions must be processed, what reports are needed, and what integrations are required.

(3) Design phase — Service (b): custom software design and system architecture. The provider specifies the technical solution: database structure, system interfaces, user interface wireframes, and integration architecture. Example: designing the data flow between a CRM and an existing ERP.

(4) Development/Selection and configuration phase — Service (c): software development and system integration. The provider builds custom modules or configures a vendor solution (e.g. SAP, Salesforce) and integrates it with existing systems. Example: configuring ERP modules and connecting them to the legacy accounting system.

(5) Testing phase — Service (d): QA testing and user acceptance testing (UAT). The provider verifies the system meets all requirements before go-live. Unit testing, integration testing, and end-user scenario testing are conducted. Example: simulating order processing end-to-end to confirm data flows correctly.

(6) Implementation phase — Service (e): deployment, data migration, and training. The provider manages the go-live: migrating data from legacy systems, deploying the new system, and training users. This is typically the most risk-heavy phase and requires strong change management.

(7) Operation and maintenance phase — Service (f): ongoing support, maintenance, and updates. After go-live the provider monitors system performance, fixes bugs, applies security patches, and enhances functionality as business needs evolve. Example: adding a new reporting module six months after launch.
key_points: Name all major IS life cycle phases: planning, requirements analysis, design, development/selection, testing, implementation, operation and maintenance
Map each service from the guest speaker correctly to the appropriate phase
Provide at least one concrete example per phase
Note that phases are sequential but may be iterative in Agile approaches
Highlight implementation as the highest-risk phase (change management, data migration)

questions.question: Explain the make vs. buy decision in IS management. What are the key factors that influence whether an organisation should develop a custom system or purchase a vendor solution?
questions.model_answer:
The make vs. buy decision determines whether an organisation builds custom IS internally or purchases and configures an existing vendor solution. 

Factors favouring Buy (vendor solution): lower initial cost and faster deployment; proven functionality with established vendor support; reduced technical risk (battle-tested in other organisations); easier access to updates and new features; standard processes enforced (can be a benefit if processes need improvement). Factors favouring Make (custom development): unique business requirements not met by market solutions; critical competitive differentiation that should not be replicated by competitors; full control over functionality and roadmap; no licensing dependency.

The vendor selection process for Buy decisions involves: defining requirements clearly (RFP — Request for Proposal), evaluating vendors against functional and non-functional criteria, assessing vendor financial stability and support capability, conducting reference checks and demos, and negotiating contracts covering SLAs, pricing, and exit clauses.

Most large organisations use a hybrid approach: buy standard ERP for back-office processes (finance, HR) and build custom solutions for genuinely differentiating customer-facing capabilities.
key_points: Define the make vs buy decision
At least 3 factors favouring buy
At least 3 factors favouring make
Describe vendor selection process (RFP)
Note that hybrid approach is common

questions.question: Distinguish between Transaction Processing Systems (TPS), Management Information Systems (MIS), and Decision Support Systems (DSS). For each, describe the type of decisions supported, the users served, and the kind of information output produced.
questions.model_answer: TPS support operational-level decisions by processing routine day-to-day transactions (sales orders, payments, inventory updates). Users are operational staff. Output is detailed transaction records and operational reports. MIS aggregate TPS data into structured periodic reports for middle management (weekly sales reports, inventory summaries). They support structured, recurring decisions with predefined formats. DSS support semi-structured decisions by providing analytical tools, modelling capabilities, and what-if analysis. Users are middle to senior managers facing decisions where judgement and data analysis combine. Output is flexible analytical output rather than fixed reports. At the apex, Executive Support Systems (ESS) present summarised, external, and internal data in dashboard form for unstructured strategic decisions by senior executives.
key_points: Correct definition and user group for TPS
Correct definition and user group for MIS
Correct definition and user group for DSS
Note the difference in decision structure (structured → semi-structured → unstructured)
ESS/EIS mentioned as executive tier

questions.question: A logistics company processes thousands of shipment bookings daily and generates weekly reports for operations managers, but struggles to provide its CEO with a real-time overview of company-wide performance. Identify which type of IS is present at each level and what IS solution would address the CEO's need. Explain your reasoning.
questions.model_answer: The shipment booking system is a Transaction Processing System (TPS) — it handles high-volume, routine operational transactions. The weekly operations reports represent a Management Information System (MIS) function — aggregating TPS data into structured reports for middle management. The CEO's need for real-time company-wide performance is an Executive Support System (ESS/EIS) requirement. An ESS would pull summarised KPIs (revenue, on-time delivery rate, cost per shipment) from the underlying systems, present them in a dashboard format, and allow drilling down on specific metrics. The justification: CEOs need broad, summarised, real-time views rather than detailed transaction data or fixed-format operational reports.
key_points: Correctly identify TPS for booking system
Correctly identify MIS for weekly reports
Correctly identify ESS/EIS as the solution for the CEO
Describe ESS characteristics (dashboard, KPIs, drill-down capability)
Justify why ESS rather than MIS or DSS for executive level

questions.question: Explain the concept of IS alignment with business strategy. Why is alignment important, and what are the main consequences of misalignment? Use a concrete example to illustrate your answer.
questions.model_answer: IS alignment means that the IS strategy, architecture, and investment priorities support and enable the business strategy. Alignment is critical because IS investments are large and long-lived — a misaligned system cannot easily be changed without major cost. Consequences of misalignment include: wasted investment (systems that do not support business needs), competitive disadvantage (competitors with aligned IS execute faster), operational inefficiency (manual workarounds for IS gaps), and inability to scale (systems that cannot grow with the business). Example: a retailer pursuing an omnichannel strategy that invests only in separate in-store and online systems (without integration) faces misalignment — the IS cannot deliver the seamless customer experience the business strategy requires. Conversely, investing in a unified inventory and customer data platform aligns IS with the omnichannel strategy.
key_points: Clear definition of IS-business alignment
Explanation of why alignment matters (investment size, longevity)
At least 2-3 consequences of misalignment
Concrete illustrative example
Note that alignment requires ongoing management, not a one-time exercise

questions.question: The Lenzing case illustrates common challenges in large-scale ERP implementation. Describe three major challenges that arose during Lenzing's ERP project and explain how each could have been mitigated through better project management or change management practices.
questions.model_answer: Lenzing, an Austrian speciality fibre manufacturer, implemented SAP ERP across complex global operations. Key challenges typically seen in such implementations: (1) Data quality and migration: historical data from legacy systems required extensive cleaning before SAP go-live. Mitigation: start data cleansing early, before ERP configuration, with a dedicated data governance team. (2) Process standardisation vs local adaptation: ERP systems enforce standard processes but Lenzing's plants had localised practices. Mitigation: conduct process workshops early to agree which processes should be standardised globally vs locally adapted, using the ERP's configuration options. (3) User resistance and training: end users comfortable with legacy systems resist change. Mitigation: involve users in requirements definition (increasing buy-in), provide role-specific training, and designate super-users as internal champions on each site.
key_points: Name at least 3 specific ERP implementation challenges
Each challenge linked to Lenzing or ERP context (not generic)
Mitigation for each challenge
Data quality / migration mentioned
Change management / user resistance mentioned

questions.question: Why do large ERP implementations so frequently experience cost overruns and timeline delays? Explain the structural reasons and describe what governance mechanisms can reduce these risks.
questions.model_answer: ERP implementations are structurally prone to overruns because: (1) scope creep — business units add requirements after project start, expanding scope without proportional budget increases; (2) underestimated complexity — legacy data migration, custom integrations, and process redesign are consistently underestimated in initial assessments; (3) resource contention — business staff needed for requirements and testing are also running daily operations, creating bottlenecks; (4) vendor and consultant dependencies — external consultants drive costs and have incentives to extend scope. Governance mechanisms: strong project steering committee with authority to reject scope additions; a formal change control process requiring sign-off for any scope change; dedicated project team members (not shared with operations); phased implementation (go-live in waves) to limit risk; and independent project health checks at key milestones.
key_points: Scope creep as a reason
Underestimation of complexity (data migration, integrations)
Resource contention between project and operations
At least 3 governance mechanisms
Phased implementation as risk reduction approach

questions.question: What is meant by 'process fit' in the context of ERP implementation? How should an organisation handle situations where the standard ERP process does not fit its business requirements?
questions.model_answer: Process fit refers to the degree to which the standard processes embedded in an ERP system match the organisation's existing or desired business processes. High fit means the ERP can be configured (not customised) to support the process with minimal change. Low fit creates a choice: (1) Adapt the business process to match the ERP standard — often the recommended approach, as ERP vendors embed industry best practices and customisation is expensive to maintain; (2) Customise the ERP — modify the system code to fit the business process, which is costly, complicates upgrades, and creates technical debt; (3) Use a bolt-on application — implement a separate specialised system integrated with the ERP for the non-standard process. Best practice: evaluate process fit during the selection phase (using fit/gap analysis), prefer process adaptation for non-differentiating processes, and limit customisation to genuinely differentiating capabilities.
key_points: Define process fit
Three options: adapt process, customise ERP, bolt-on
Explain why process adaptation is generally preferred
Note the cost and upgrade risk of customisation
Fit/gap analysis as the assessment tool

questions.question: Define change management in the context of IS implementation. What are the key activities of an effective change management programme, and why is it considered as important as the technical implementation itself?
questions.model_answer: Change management in IS implementation refers to the structured approach to transitioning people from current work practices to new IS-supported processes. It addresses the human side of technology adoption. Key activities: (1) Stakeholder analysis — identify who is affected, their level of impact, and their likely resistance or support; (2) Communication plan — explain why the change is happening, what it means for each group, and what support is available; (3) Training — role-specific training on the new system and processes; (4) Super-user network — designate internal champions who can support colleagues during and after go-live; (5) Resistance management — identify root causes of resistance and address them through involvement, incentives, or leadership intervention. Change management is as important as technical implementation because even a technically perfect system fails if users do not adopt it. The most common cause of IS failure is not technical malfunction but poor user adoption.
key_points: Clear definition of change management
Stakeholder analysis
Communication plan
Training (role-specific)
Super-user / change champion network
Note that poor adoption is the leading cause of IS failure

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'big bang' vs phased ERP go-live strategy. What are the advantages and risks of each approach, and what organisational factors should guide the choice?
questions.model_answer: Big bang go-live: the entire organisation switches to the new ERP simultaneously on a single date. Advantages: eliminates integration complexity between old and new systems (only one system active); faster realisation of benefits; no need to maintain parallel systems. Risks: extremely high operational risk — a problem at go-live affects the entire business simultaneously; intensive preparation and testing requirements; if go-live fails, rollback is very disruptive. Phased go-live: implementation proceeds in waves — by geography, business unit, or process module. Advantages: limits risk to each phase; learning from early phases improves subsequent ones; smaller, more manageable scope per go-live. Risks: complex integrations between live and legacy systems during transition; prolonged implementation timeline; uneven adoption across the organisation. Guiding factors: organisation size (larger = phased preferred), geographic dispersion (phased by country common), system complexity, risk appetite, and business continuity requirements.
key_points: Define big bang and phased approaches
2+ advantages and risks for each
Note integration complexity in phased
Note operational risk concentration in big bang
State at least 2 organisational factors that guide the choice

questions.question: Define an ERP system and explain its core architectural characteristics. Why is the integrated single-database architecture considered the key differentiator of ERP compared to standalone functional applications?
questions.model_answer: An ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) system is an integrated software suite that manages and coordinates all core business processes across an organisation using a unified database. Core architectural characteristics: (1) Single integrated database — all modules share one data store, eliminating data duplication and synchronisation problems; (2) Modular structure — functional modules (Finance, HR, SCM, Manufacturing, etc.) can be implemented selectively; (3) Process integration — business processes span modules automatically (a sales order triggers inventory reservation, production planning, and AR creation without manual transfer); (4) Real-time data — transactions are immediately visible across all functions; (5) Configurable — parameters can be set without changing code. The integrated single-database architecture is the key differentiator because it eliminates the 'islands of information' problem of legacy systems: no data inconsistencies, no batch synchronisation delays, no manual reconciliation between systems. This integration enables automated end-to-end processes and provides management with a single source of truth.
key_points: Clear ERP definition
Single integrated database as key characteristic
Modular structure explained
Real-time processing explained
Explain why integration solves 'islands of information' problem
Contrast with standalone functional applications

questions.question: A manufacturing company is evaluating whether to implement an ERP system. What are the main expected benefits and the main risks of ERP adoption? How should the company structure its business case?
questions.model_answer: Expected benefits of ERP: (1) Process efficiency — automation of manual data entry and reconciliation; (2) Data quality and consistency — single source of truth eliminates duplication; (3) Visibility — real-time reporting across functions; (4) Compliance — built-in controls and audit trails support regulatory requirements; (5) Scalability — system can grow with the business without redesign. Main risks: (1) Implementation cost and overrun — ERP projects frequently exceed budget and timeline; (2) Business disruption during go-live; (3) Scope creep; (4) User resistance; (5) Vendor lock-in. Business case structure: (a) Baseline assessment — current process costs and pain points; (b) Benefit quantification — estimated savings/revenue impact (process hours saved, error reduction, inventory reduction); (c) Cost estimate — licensing, implementation, training, internal resources; (d) NPV/payback analysis; (e) Risk assessment and mitigation plan. The business case must be approved at board level given the scale of investment and business disruption involved.
key_points: At least 4 ERP benefits
At least 3 ERP risks
Business case components: baseline, benefits, costs
Mention NPV or payback calculation
Risk assessment as part of the business case

questions.question: Distinguish between ERP customisation, configuration, and extension. Why do most ERP consultants recommend minimising customisation, and what are the long-term consequences of heavy customisation?
questions.model_answer: Configuration involves adjusting system parameters within the ERP's built-in flexibility — setting tax rates, defining account codes, enabling or disabling standard features. No code change. Configuration is always reversible and upgrade-safe. Extension (bolt-on) adds new functionality via certified third-party applications that interface with the ERP through standard APIs. Avoids core code changes while extending capability. Customisation modifies the ERP's core code to change standard behaviour. Consultants recommend minimising customisation because: (1) It is expensive to develop and test; (2) It must be re-applied or redeveloped with each ERP upgrade, making upgrades much more costly; (3) It creates technical debt — complexity accumulates over time; (4) Custom code is harder to support and document; (5) Standard processes often represent industry best practice — departing from them without good reason is risky. Long-term consequences of heavy customisation: inability to upgrade without major cost (organisations get stuck on old versions), increasing maintenance burden, and difficulty hiring consultants familiar with non-standard implementations.
key_points: Clear distinction between configuration, extension, and customisation
Configuration: parameter-based, no code change
Extension: third-party bolt-on via APIs
Customisation: core code modification
At least 3 reasons to minimise customisation
Upgrade impact as key consequence

questions.question: What is cloud ERP and how does it differ from on-premise ERP deployment? What factors should guide an organisation's choice between cloud and on-premise?
questions.model_answer: On-premise ERP is installed on the organisation's own servers, managed by internal IT staff. The organisation owns the licence, controls the infrastructure, and is responsible for upgrades, backups, and security. Cloud ERP (SaaS model) is hosted by the vendor on shared or dedicated cloud infrastructure, accessed via the internet, with the vendor managing infrastructure, updates, and security. The organisation pays a subscription rather than a capital licence fee. Key differences: (1) Capex vs Opex — on-premise is capital-intensive upfront; cloud converts to operating expense; (2) Customisation — on-premise allows deep customisation; cloud limits customisation to configuration (protecting the vendor's ability to upgrade all clients simultaneously); (3) Upgrades — cloud upgrades are automatic and continuous; on-premise requires managed upgrade projects; (4) Integration — on-premise offers more control over integrations with legacy systems. Factors guiding choice: data sovereignty and compliance requirements (on-premise for regulated data), IT capability (cloud reduces internal IT burden), capital budget constraints (cloud preferred), customisation needs (heavy customisers favour on-premise), and competitive differentiation from IS (higher differentiation → more reason for on-premise control).
key_points: Clear definition of cloud vs on-premise
Capex vs Opex distinction
Customisation limitations in cloud
Upgrade model difference
At least 3 factors guiding the choice
Data sovereignty/compliance mentioned

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'best of breed' vs 'single vendor' IS strategy. What are the trade-offs, and in what organisational contexts is each approach most appropriate?
questions.model_answer: Single vendor strategy: the organisation uses one ERP vendor's suite for all core functions (e.g. SAP for finance, HR, SCM, manufacturing). Advantages: native integration (no interface development), single support contract, consistent user experience, vendor accountability for end-to-end processes. Disadvantages: no single vendor excels in every functional area; organisation is locked into one vendor's roadmap. Best of breed strategy: the organisation selects the best available specialised application for each function (e.g. Salesforce for CRM, Workday for HR, SAP for Finance, a specialised WMS for warehouse management). Advantages: best functionality in each area; competitive leverage across multiple vendors. Disadvantages: complex integration layer required; multiple vendor relationships; data consistency across systems requires active management (middleware, APIs). Appropriate contexts: single vendor suits organisations that prefer simplicity, have standard processes, or lack strong IT integration capability; best of breed suits organisations with highly specialised or differentiating processes in specific functions and strong IT integration capability.
key_points: Clear definition of both strategies
2+ advantages and disadvantages for each
Integration complexity as key risk of best of breed
Vendor lock-in as key risk of single vendor
Context where each is appropriate

questions.question: Describe the Finance and Controlling (FI/CO) module in SAP or a comparable ERP. What are its main sub-components, what business processes does it automate, and how does it integrate with other ERP modules?
questions.model_answer: The Finance and Controlling module covers financial accounting and management accounting. Financial Accounting (FI) sub-components: General Ledger (records all financial transactions, basis for financial statements), Accounts Receivable (customer invoices, receipts, credit management), Accounts Payable (supplier invoices, payment processing, cash flow), Fixed Assets (capitalisation, depreciation, disposal). Controlling (CO) sub-components: Cost Centre Accounting, Profit Centre Accounting, Product Costing, Internal Orders. Business processes automated: period-end close (automatic journal postings), payment runs, VAT reporting, depreciation calculations, cost allocation. Integration with other modules: a goods receipt in Materials Management (MM) triggers an automatic accounting posting in FI; a sales invoice in Sales and Distribution (SD) automatically posts to FI-AR and updates revenue accounts in CO; payroll from Human Resources posts to FI. This seamless integration eliminates manual inter-departmental data transfers and ensures real-time financial visibility.
key_points: Distinction between FI (financial accounting) and CO (management accounting)
Key FI sub-modules: GL, AR, AP, Fixed Assets
Key CO sub-modules: cost centres, profit centres, product costing
At least 2 automatic integration examples with other modules
Note real-time visibility as benefit of integration

questions.question: Explain the Supply Chain Management (SCM) and Materials Management (MM) modules in ERP. What are the key processes covered, and how do they contribute to inventory optimisation and supplier management?
questions.model_answer: Materials Management (MM) covers procurement and inventory management within the ERP. Key processes: purchase requisition (user request for material), purchase order (formal order to supplier), goods receipt (recording delivery), invoice verification (matching PO, goods receipt, and supplier invoice — the '3-way match'), and inventory management (stock movements, physical inventory). Supply Chain Management (SCM) extends to planning and coordination across the supply chain: demand planning (forecasting), production planning (MRP — Material Requirements Planning calculates what to produce and when), supply network planning (multi-site coordination), and transportation management. Contribution to inventory optimisation: MRP calculates optimal order quantities and timing based on demand forecasts and current stock levels, reducing both excess inventory and stockouts. Contribution to supplier management: purchase order workflow, supplier evaluation scorecards, and automatic 3-way match speed up procurement and identify invoice discrepancies. Integration: MM feeds production orders into manufacturing modules and triggers FI postings on goods receipt and invoice verification.
key_points: Distinction between MM (procurement/inventory) and SCM (planning/coordination)
Procurement process: requisition → PO → goods receipt → invoice verification (3-way match)
MRP explained and its role in inventory optimisation
Supplier management features
Integration with FI and production modules

questions.question: What is the Human Resources (HR) module in an ERP system? Describe its main functional areas and explain how HR integration with finance and payroll eliminates common data quality problems in unintegrated systems.
questions.model_answer: The HR module manages the employee lifecycle from recruitment to retirement. Main functional areas: Personnel Administration (employee master data: personal details, contracts, positions, organisation chart), Payroll (calculating gross pay, deductions, net pay, and mandatory statutory contributions), Time and Attendance (recording working hours, leave, overtime), Benefits Administration (pensions, health insurance, expense policies), Recruitment (applicant tracking, onboarding), and Training and Development. Integration with Finance: payroll postings from HR-Payroll automatically create accounting entries in FI (payroll cost allocation by cost centre in CO). This integration eliminates common data quality problems: in unintegrated systems, HR and Finance maintain separate employee records that diverge over time (starters not entered in finance, leavers not removed from payroll — causing ghost employee payments). Single-database integration ensures that when an employee's record is created or changed in HR, it is immediately reflected in all payroll and financial reports.
key_points: Main HR functional areas: personnel admin, payroll, time & attendance, benefits
Explain automatic payroll-to-finance posting
Cost centre allocation in Controlling via HR
Ghost employee / data consistency problem in unintegrated systems
Single database eliminates HR-Finance data divergence

questions.question: What is Sales and Distribution (SD) in ERP, and how does the order-to-cash process work end-to-end within an integrated ERP system? Identify all the modules touched by this process.
questions.model_answer: Sales and Distribution (SD) manages the customer-facing side of the business: pricing, orders, delivery, and billing. The order-to-cash (O2C) process in an integrated ERP: (1) Sales order creation (SD): customer order entered with products, quantities, delivery date, and pricing — automatically checks credit limit (FI-AR) and product availability (MM/Inventory); (2) Delivery creation (SD/WM): warehouse management module picks, packs, and posts goods issue, reducing stock (MM inventory) and triggering a cost-of-goods-sold posting (FI); (3) Transport and shipment: logistics execution records transport details; (4) Billing (SD/FI): invoice generated in SD automatically creates an accounts receivable item in FI-AR and revenue posting in CO; (5) Cash receipt (FI-AR): payment received from customer clears the AR open item. Modules touched: SD (order, delivery, billing), MM/WM (inventory, warehouse), FI (credit check, AR, revenue posting), CO (profitability analysis). The integration means no manual data transfer between departments — one transaction triggers postings across all relevant modules.
key_points: Clear definition of SD module
Order-to-cash process steps in sequence
Credit check via FI-AR integration
Goods issue triggers inventory (MM) and cost posting (FI)
Billing triggers AR and revenue posting automatically
All modules identified: SD, MM/WM, FI, CO

questions.question: What is Manufacturing Execution and Production Planning in ERP? Explain how MRP (Material Requirements Planning) works and how production orders interact with other ERP modules.
questions.model_answer: Production Planning (PP) in ERP manages manufacturing processes from demand-based planning to production order completion. Material Requirements Planning (MRP) is the core algorithm: it takes confirmed demand (sales orders, forecast) and explodes it through the Bill of Materials (BOM) to calculate what materials are needed, in what quantities, and when. MRP compares requirements against current stock and open purchase orders, generating purchase requisitions for shortfalls and planned production orders for in-house manufactured components. Production orders: when released, reserve materials from MM inventory; as work proceeds, production confirmations record actual labour and machine time (HR/CO); on completion, goods receipt of finished product updates inventory (MM) and triggers cost postings (CO — comparing standard vs actual cost). Integration points: PP receives demand signals from SD (sales orders) and sends purchase requisitions to MM procurement; actual costs flow to CO for variance analysis; capacity planning integrates with HR scheduling. MRP thus coordinates procurement, production, and finance in a closed loop.
key_points: MRP definition and algorithm: demand → BOM explosion → requirements calculation
Comparison of requirements vs stock → purchase requisitions or planned orders
Production order lifecycle: release → materials reservation → confirmation → goods receipt
Integration with SD (demand input), MM (materials), CO (cost variance), HR (capacity)
BOM (Bill of Materials) role explained

questions.question: Describe the main criteria used to evaluate IS investment decisions. How do organisations weigh technical, strategic, and financial criteria when selecting among competing IS investment options?
questions.model_answer: IS investment decisions require multi-dimensional evaluation. Financial criteria: ROI (return on investment), NPV (net present value of expected cash flows), payback period, and total cost of ownership (TCO — including licence, implementation, training, and ongoing support costs). Financial criteria translate IS benefits into comparable monetary terms. Technical criteria: alignment with existing architecture, scalability, security, integration capability, vendor stability and support, and technology longevity (avoiding obsolescence). Strategic criteria: alignment with business strategy (does this IS enable the capabilities required by the strategy?), competitive differentiation (does it build capabilities competitors cannot easily replicate?), regulatory compliance (does it meet legal and reporting requirements?). Organisations typically use a weighted scoring model: each criterion is assigned a weight reflecting its importance; each option is scored against each criterion; the weighted total determines ranking. However, some criteria are threshold requirements (e.g. security compliance) that must be met regardless of overall score. The final decision should be reviewed by a steering committee combining IT, business, and finance perspectives.
key_points: Financial criteria: ROI, NPV, payback, TCO
Technical criteria: scalability, integration, vendor stability
Strategic criteria: business alignment, competitive differentiation, compliance
Weighted scoring model as decision tool
Steering committee / governance for final decision

questions.question: What is IS governance and why is it important? Describe the main IS governance mechanisms and explain how they prevent poor IS investment decisions.
questions.model_answer: IS governance is the framework of policies, processes, roles, and decision rights that ensure IS investments and operations align with organisational strategy and deliver value. It is important because IS decisions involve large sums, long implementation timescales, and significant business risk — poor governance leads to runaway projects, misaligned systems, and wasted investment. Main governance mechanisms: (1) IS Steering Committee — senior body (CIO, CFO, business unit heads) that approves major IS investments and resolves strategic trade-offs; (2) Project portfolio management — formal process for prioritising competing IS projects against strategy and resource constraints; (3) Change control process — mandatory sign-off procedure for any scope changes during projects; (4) IS architecture review — ensures new systems align with enterprise architecture and technical standards; (5) Post-implementation review — evaluates whether IS delivered promised benefits, informing future investment decisions. Governance prevents poor decisions by: ensuring decisions are made at the right organisational level (strategic vs operational), requiring explicit benefit justification, and creating accountability for outcomes.
key_points: Clear definition of IS governance
Why governance matters (investment scale, risk)
IS Steering Committee
Portfolio management / project prioritisation
Change control process
Post-implementation review
How governance prevents specific failure modes

questions.question: Explain the difference between structured, semi-structured, and unstructured IS decisions. Give one example of each and identify which IS type best supports each decision level.
questions.model_answer: Structured decisions are fully programmable: the decision rules, inputs, and outputs are known and well-defined. Example: automatically reordering stock when inventory falls below reorder point. Supported by TPS and rule-based automation — no human judgement required. Semi-structured decisions have some defined parameters but also require human judgement. Example: deciding whether to approve a customer credit limit extension — some rules apply (current balance, credit score) but also requires judgement about relationship and context. Supported by DSS (Decision Support Systems) which provide data, models, and what-if analysis to inform human judgement. Unstructured decisions are highly complex, unique, and data-dependent in ways that cannot be pre-specified. Example: deciding whether to enter a new international market. Supported by ESS (Executive Support Systems) that aggregate external and internal data in flexible formats, and by expert systems or AI-assisted analysis. The level of IS support decreases as decision structure decreases — IS can automate structured decisions but only support (not replace) judgement for unstructured ones.
key_points: Clear distinction between structured, semi-structured, unstructured
One valid example for each
TPS supports structured decisions
DSS supports semi-structured decisions
ESS supports unstructured decisions
Note IS supports but cannot replace human judgement for unstructured decisions

questions.question: What is the role of the Chief Information Officer (CIO) in IS governance and strategic alignment? How has the CIO role evolved from technical management to business leadership?
questions.model_answer: The CIO is responsible for IS strategy, governance, and the alignment of technology with business goals. In the IS governance structure, the CIO typically chairs or is a key member of the IS Steering Committee, owns the IS strategy document, and is accountable for IS investment performance. Evolution of the CIO role: traditionally (1980s–1990s), the CIO was primarily a technical manager responsible for keeping systems running, managing IT infrastructure, and overseeing software development teams. This was a back-office, cost-control function. From the 2000s onward, as IS became central to competitive strategy (e-commerce, digital transformation, data analytics), the CIO evolved into a business leader who contributes to strategic decisions, sponsors digital transformation initiatives, manages partnerships with technology vendors, and translates business needs into IS investments. Modern CIOs must be bilingual — technically credible with IT teams and business-credible with the board. In many organisations, this evolution has split into CIO (run existing IS infrastructure) and Chief Digital Officer (CDO) for digital transformation and innovation.
key_points: CIO's governance role: steering committee, IS strategy ownership, investment accountability
Historical CIO as technical manager
Evolution to business leadership role
Digital transformation as driver of role change
Bilingual requirement: technical + business credibility
CIO/CDO split in some organisations

questions.question: What are the main criteria for prioritising IS projects in an organisation's IS portfolio? How should an IS steering committee choose between multiple competing investment proposals?
questions.model_answer: IS project prioritisation criteria: (1) Strategic alignment — how directly does the project support the business strategy? Projects enabling critical strategic capabilities get higher priority; (2) Financial return — NPV, payback period, and ROI rank projects by value delivered relative to investment; (3) Risk — technical risk (new technology, complex integration), execution risk (project team capability), and business risk (operational disruption during implementation); (4) Interdependencies — some projects are prerequisites for others (infrastructure must precede applications); (5) Regulatory and compliance — mandatory projects with legal deadlines are non-negotiable and take priority regardless of ROI; (6) Resource availability — available IT and business capacity constrains how many projects can run simultaneously. IS steering committee process: collect project proposals from all business units using a standard template (objectives, costs, benefits, timeline, risk); score each proposal against weighted criteria; present ranked portfolio to steering committee for discussion; resolve competing priorities through explicit trade-off discussion involving all business unit heads; approve portfolio within resource and budget constraints. Review annually or when strategic priorities change.
key_points: Strategic alignment as primary criterion
Financial return (NPV, ROI, payback)
Risk assessment dimension
Mandatory/compliance projects as non-negotiable
Resource capacity as constraint
Standard proposal template and weighted scoring process

questions.question: Compare the Waterfall and Agile methodologies for IS development and implementation. In what contexts is each most appropriate, and what are their respective failure modes?
questions.model_answer: Waterfall methodology is a sequential, phase-gated approach: requirements → design → development → testing → deployment, with each phase completed before the next begins. Strengths: clear milestones, full specification before development, easier contract management for fixed-scope projects, well-suited for stable requirements and regulated environments (auditors can verify each phase). Failure modes: late discovery of requirements errors (not found until testing), inability to adapt to changing business needs, 'big bang' delivery means users see the system only at go-live. Agile methodology delivers in iterative sprints (2-4 weeks), involving users continuously and adapting requirements based on feedback. Strengths: early and continuous user feedback, adaptability to changing requirements, partial value delivered early, lower risk of catastrophic failure. Failure modes: scope creep if product backlog is poorly managed, difficulty in fixed-budget contracts, requires sustained business user availability, and works less well when full specification is legally required upfront. Most appropriate contexts: Waterfall for large infrastructure projects, compliance-driven systems, and stable requirements; Agile for business applications with evolving requirements, user-facing systems, and innovation projects.
key_points: Correct definition of Waterfall phases
Correct definition of Agile sprints and iteration
2+ strengths and failure modes for each
Context where each is appropriate
Note Agile requires continuous user involvement

questions.question: What are the key activities in the IS implementation phase? Why is the go-live transition considered the highest-risk moment in an IS project, and what specific practices reduce go-live risk?
questions.model_answer: Key implementation phase activities: data migration (extracting, cleansing, transforming, and loading historical data from legacy systems into the new system), user acceptance testing (UAT — business users validate that the system meets requirements with realistic data), training (user education on new processes and system), cutover planning (detailed sequence of go-live steps), and parallel running (running old and new systems simultaneously during transition to catch discrepancies). Go-live is highest-risk because: all operational processes switch simultaneously to a new system that users may not know well, on real data, with real business consequences. Legacy system fallback may not be possible. Practices reducing go-live risk: extensive UAT with real business scenarios before go-live; dress rehearsal (mock go-live with complete cutover sequence); dedicated hypercare support team available immediately post go-live; clear rollback plan if critical problems emerge within 24-48 hours; phased go-live (by geography or module) to limit scope; and war room (all key project team members available on site at go-live).
key_points: Data migration activities (extract, cleanse, load)
UAT with real business scenarios
Training before go-live
Cutover plan and parallel running
Why go-live is highest risk
At least 3 specific go-live risk reduction practices
Hypercare / war room concept

questions.question: What is data migration in IS implementation? Describe the main steps in a data migration project and explain the most common data quality issues encountered.
questions.model_answer: Data migration is the process of moving data from legacy systems to a new IS, ensuring accuracy, completeness, and consistency. Main steps: (1) Data mapping — identify which data elements in the source system correspond to which fields in the target system; (2) Data extraction — extract data from legacy sources (databases, spreadsheets, paper records scanned); (3) Data cleansing — identify and correct quality issues (duplicates, invalid values, missing fields, format inconsistencies); (4) Data transformation — convert formats, currencies, date conventions, and coding structures to match the target system's requirements; (5) Data loading — insert cleaned, transformed data into the new system; (6) Reconciliation — verify that all expected records loaded correctly (record counts, control totals, sample checks). Common data quality issues: duplicate customer or supplier records (created over years without deduplication), missing mandatory fields (e.g. date of birth for employees not previously required), inconsistent formatting (different address formats), obsolete records (inactive customers, retired products, former employees still in system), and orphan records (transactions without matching master data). Poor data quality is one of the leading causes of ERP go-live failure.
key_points: Clear definition of data migration
Steps: mapping, extraction, cleansing, transformation, loading, reconciliation
At least 3 common data quality issues
Note that data quality is a major go-live risk
Deduplication as a key cleansing activity

questions.question: Define IS feasibility study and describe the four main types of feasibility assessment. Why is a structured feasibility study essential before committing to IS investment?
questions.model_answer: A feasibility study is a preliminary analysis conducted before full IS project approval to assess whether the proposed system can and should be built. The four main feasibility dimensions: (1) Technical feasibility — can the required system be built with available or acquirable technology? Assesses technical complexity, existing infrastructure compatibility, and team capability. (2) Economic feasibility — are the financial benefits worth the costs? Includes cost estimation, benefit identification, ROI/NPV calculation, and payback analysis. (3) Organisational feasibility — is the organisation ready to adopt and use the system? Assesses user acceptance likelihood, management support, change capability, and process readiness. (4) Schedule feasibility — can the system be delivered within the required timeframe? Assesses resource availability, critical path, and external dependencies. A structured feasibility study is essential because: committing to full IS development without feasibility assessment risks investing millions in systems that are technically impossible, economically unjustifiable, organisationally rejected, or delivered too late to be useful. The feasibility study is the cheapest way to discover these problems — before resources are fully committed.
key_points: Definition of feasibility study
Technical feasibility
Economic feasibility (costs vs benefits)
Organisational feasibility (readiness, acceptance)
Schedule feasibility (timeline, resources)
Why feasibility study is cost-effective risk reduction

questions.question: A mid-sized food distribution company wants to implement a new warehouse management system (WMS) to replace manual stock tracking. Walk through a complete feasibility assessment for this project, covering all four feasibility dimensions.
questions.model_answer: Technical feasibility: modern WMS systems are commercially available from multiple vendors (Manhattan Associates, SAP EWM, Oracle WMS), so the technology exists and does not require novel development. Assessment must check: integration capability with existing ERP/finance system (API availability), hardware requirements (barcode scanners, RF terminals, labels), network coverage in warehouse, and internal IT team's implementation capability. Result: feasible if integration is standard. Economic feasibility: costs include WMS licence (or SaaS subscription), implementation consulting, hardware (scanners), training, and internal IT time. Benefits include: reduced picking errors (fewer customer returns and credits), lower headcount for manual stock reconciliation, faster order processing (revenue impact), and reduced inventory write-offs through better visibility. NPV analysis using realistic benefit estimates and 3-year horizon. Organisational feasibility: warehouse staff may resist change from manual processes. Requires management sponsorship, super-user identification, and involvement of warehouse supervisors in requirements definition. Schedule feasibility: standard WMS implementation takes 3-9 months. Key dependencies: hardware procurement lead time, data migration (product master data cleaning), and peak season avoidance (do not go live during Christmas if food distribution peaks then).
key_points: Applies all four feasibility dimensions to the specific case
Technical: existing technology + integration check
Economic: named costs AND benefits
Organisational: user resistance + management sponsorship
Schedule: realistic timeline + peak season consideration
Shows reasoning for feasibility conclusion in each dimension

questions.question: What is the difference between functional and non-functional requirements in IS? Give examples of each and explain why both must be captured in a feasibility study and requirements specification.
questions.model_answer: Functional requirements define WHAT the system must do — the specific features, processes, and data it must support. Examples: 'The system must allow users to search for a customer by name or account number'; 'The system must generate a VAT invoice on order completion'; 'The system must send automated email confirmation to customers on order placement'. Non-functional requirements define HOW WELL the system must perform — the quality attributes of the system. Examples: Performance ('The system must respond to search queries in under 2 seconds for 95% of requests'); Availability ('The system must be available 99.9% of working hours'); Security ('All personal data must be encrypted at rest and in transit'); Scalability ('The system must support up to 500 concurrent users without performance degradation'); Auditability ('All transactions must be logged with user ID, timestamp, and before/after values'). Both must be captured because: systems that meet functional requirements but fail non-functional ones are operationally inadequate (a system that does everything but is too slow is unusable); non-functional requirements often drive significant architectural and cost differences between vendor solutions.
key_points: Clear definition of functional requirements
Clear definition of non-functional requirements
2+ examples of each
Performance, availability, security, scalability as non-functional categories
Why both types are necessary for complete requirements

questions.question: What are the key components of a Request for Proposal (RFP) for IS procurement? How should responses be evaluated to select the best vendor?
questions.model_answer: A Request for Proposal (RFP) is a formal document issued to potential vendors describing the organisation's IS requirements and requesting detailed proposals. Key RFP components: (1) Organisation background — company size, industry, current IS landscape; (2) Scope of requirements — functional requirements (what the system must do), non-functional requirements (performance, security), and integration requirements; (3) Technical environment — existing infrastructure and integration constraints; (4) Commercial terms — budget range (optional), contract type, SLA expectations, implementation timeline; (5) Vendor information required — company profile, financial stability, client references, implementation methodology, pricing model; (6) Evaluation criteria — how responses will be scored (weighting of price, functionality, vendor stability, etc.); (7) Response format — structured format to enable consistent comparison. Vendor evaluation process: score each vendor against the criteria stated in the RFP using a weighted scoring model; shortlist 2-3 vendors for detailed demonstrations with real business scenarios; conduct reference calls with existing clients of similar size and industry; assess total cost of ownership (licence + implementation + 3-year support); negotiate with the preferred vendor before final selection.
key_points: Definition of RFP and its purpose
Key RFP sections: scope, requirements, commercial terms, evaluation criteria
Functional AND non-functional requirements in RFP
Weighted scoring model for evaluation
Vendor demonstration and reference calls as part of evaluation
TCO (total cost of ownership) assessment

questions.question: Define Net Present Value (NPV) and explain how it is applied to evaluate an IS investment. What does a positive vs negative NPV tell a decision-maker, and what are the limitations of NPV for IS evaluation?
questions.model_answer: NPV discounts all expected future cash flows from an IS investment back to present value using a discount rate (typically the organisation's cost of capital or required rate of return), then subtracts the initial investment. NPV = Sum of [Cash flow(t) / (1+r)^t] - Initial investment. A positive NPV means the investment creates value — expected returns exceed the cost of capital and the investment cost. A negative NPV means the investment destroys value — expected returns are insufficient to justify the cost. For IS investment: benefits might include labour savings (reduced headcount or overtime), revenue increases (faster order processing, fewer errors), and cost avoidance (regulatory fines avoided). Costs include licence, implementation, training, and ongoing support. Limitations of NPV for IS evaluation: (1) Benefit quantification is difficult — many IS benefits are intangible (better decision-making, improved customer experience) and cannot be reliably monetised; (2) Forecast uncertainty — IS projects frequently differ from plan, making 5-year cash flows unreliable; (3) Strategic options value — some IS investments create future capabilities whose option value NPV does not capture; (4) Risk is incorporated only crudely through the discount rate.
key_points: Correct NPV formula or verbal description
Positive NPV = value-creating investment
Negative NPV = value-destroying investment
Application to IS benefits and costs
At least 3 limitations of NPV for IS decisions
Intangible benefits and forecast uncertainty as key limitations

questions.question: What is Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) and why is it important for IS investment decisions? Describe the main cost categories included in TCO and explain how ignoring TCO leads to poor IS decisions.
questions.model_answer: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) captures all costs associated with an IS over its full lifecycle — not just the initial purchase price. It is critical for IS decisions because vendors often quote low licence prices while implementation, training, and support costs are many times higher. Main TCO categories: (1) Initial acquisition costs — licence/subscription fees, hardware; (2) Implementation costs — external consulting and project management fees, internal staff time diverted from operations, data migration; (3) Training costs — initial user training and ongoing for new joiners; (4) Operations costs — annual maintenance/support fees (typically 18-22% of licence for perpetual licence software), internal IT support headcount, hosting/infrastructure; (5) Upgrade and enhancement costs — periodic major upgrades, customisation maintenance; (6) Retirement costs — eventual decommissioning and migration to successor system. Ignoring TCO leads to poor decisions because: organisations choose the cheapest licence option only to discover that high implementation complexity (customisation) and expensive annual maintenance make it far more expensive over 5-10 years than a higher-licence alternative with lower ongoing costs. Cloud SaaS vs on-premise comparisons are particularly distorted without TCO because capex and opex cost structures are completely different.
key_points: Definition of TCO
Why low licence price is misleading
Named TCO categories: acquisition, implementation, training, operations, upgrade
Typical maintenance as percentage of licence
How ignoring TCO leads to wrong vendor selection
Cloud vs on-premise TCO comparison point

questions.question: Explain the payback period method for IS investment evaluation. What are its advantages over NPV, when is it most appropriate, and what are its key limitations?
questions.model_answer: Payback period calculates how many years it takes for the cumulative net cash flows from an IS investment to recover the initial investment. Method: sum net annual benefits until cumulative total equals initial investment. If an IS investment costs €500,000 and saves €150,000 per year, payback = 500,000 / 150,000 = 3.33 years. Advantages over NPV: simple to calculate and explain to non-financial stakeholders; intuitive measure of how quickly investment risk is recovered; particularly useful when technology obsolescence risk is high (shorter payback = lower obsolescence exposure). Most appropriate: IS investments in fast-changing technology areas, where management is particularly risk-averse, or where budget decisions require simple comparative ranking. Key limitations: (1) Ignores the time value of money (a £1 benefit in year 3 is counted the same as £1 in year 1); (2) Ignores all cash flows after payback (a project with a 2-year payback but no further benefits is ranked above a project with a 3-year payback and large benefits in years 4-10); (3) Does not indicate profitability — only speed of cost recovery. Best practice: use payback as a screening tool alongside NPV and IRR rather than as the sole criterion.
key_points: Correct definition and calculation of payback period
Numerical example demonstrating calculation
2+ advantages over NPV (simplicity, risk recovery speed, obsolescence)
3 key limitations: ignores time value, ignores post-payback, not a profitability measure
Recommendation to use as one of several tools

questions.question: How should an organisation measure and track benefits realisation after an IS implementation? Why do many IS investments fail to deliver their promised financial benefits, and what governance mechanisms prevent this?
questions.model_answer: Benefits realisation management is the practice of tracking whether promised IS benefits are actually delivered after go-live. Process: (1) Benefits identification and baseline — before implementation, define each benefit clearly (e.g. 'reduce accounts payable processing time by 30%') and measure current state baseline; (2) Benefits ownership — assign a specific business manager (not IT) as accountable for delivering each benefit; (3) Benefits tracking — at defined intervals (3, 6, 12 months post go-live), measure actual vs baseline on each benefit; (4) Benefits reporting — report to IS steering committee with variance explanations and remediation actions for underperforming benefits. Why promised benefits often fail to materialise: IT projects end at go-live, but benefit realisation requires sustained business process change that business managers (under operational pressure) deprioritise; original benefit estimates were over-optimistic to secure project approval; users work around the new system rather than adopting new processes; organisational changes during implementation mean benefit assumptions no longer apply. Governance mechanisms: mandatory benefits realisation plan as part of project approval; post-implementation review at 6-12 months with business accountable (not IT) for delivery; link IS investment decisions to outcome of previous IS benefits tracking.
key_points: Benefits realisation management defined
Baseline measurement before implementation
Business (not IT) ownership of benefits
Regular post-go-live tracking at defined intervals
At least 3 reasons benefits fail to materialise
Post-implementation review as governance mechanism

questions.question: What is an RFP (Request for Proposal) process and how does it differ from an RFI (Request for Information) and RFQ (Request for Quotation)? When should each be used in IS procurement?
questions.model_answer: RFI (Request for Information) is issued early in the procurement process to gather general information about vendor capabilities, market solutions, and rough cost ranges. It is exploratory — no commitment to purchase. Used when the organisation needs to understand the vendor landscape before defining detailed requirements. RFP (Request for Proposal) is issued when requirements are defined. It asks vendors to propose a complete solution addressing specified functional and non-functional requirements, with commercial terms. Responses are evaluated against defined criteria. Used for complex IS procurements where solution design matters, not just price. RFQ (Request for Quotation) is issued when specifications are already precisely defined and the procurement is primarily a price competition. Used for commodity procurement or when the IS specification is already fully defined (e.g. additional hardware of an existing type). Sequence in IS procurement: RFI to understand the market → RFP to select a solution and vendor → RFQ for specific component pricing within the selected solution. For complex IS (ERP, CRM, major IS projects), the full RFI → RFP → negotiation sequence is appropriate. For simple commodity IS (laptops, standard software licences), RFQ is sufficient.
key_points: RFI: exploratory, early stage, no commitment
RFP: defined requirements, solution proposal, evaluation-based selection
RFQ: fully specified, price competition
Correct sequencing of RFI → RFP → RFQ in complex procurement
When to use each in IS procurement context

questions.question: What criteria should an organisation use to evaluate IS vendors during the selection process? How should the evaluation be structured to ensure objectivity and comprehensive assessment?
questions.model_answer: IS vendor evaluation criteria: (1) Functional fit — how well the vendor's solution covers the functional requirements defined in the RFP (assessed through scripted demonstrations with the organisation's own scenarios); (2) Total Cost of Ownership — licence/subscription, implementation, training, and 5-year support costs; (3) Technical compatibility — integration with existing systems, security certifications, hosting environment; (4) Vendor stability and viability — financial strength, client base size, R&D investment (a vendor that may be acquired or fail creates long-term risk); (5) Implementation methodology and track record — how the vendor manages projects, and references from clients of similar size and industry; (6) Support model — helpdesk quality, SLA terms, escalation process, local vs remote support; (7) Scalability — can the solution grow with the organisation's anticipated growth? Evaluation structure for objectivity: use a scoring committee with business and IT representatives; score each criterion independently before group discussion; use a predefined weighted scoring template; require all vendors to use the same demonstration script and data; conduct reference calls with specific questions (not just open-ended conversation); and document scoring rationale to defend the decision if challenged.
key_points: Functional fit via scripted demonstration
