TCO as evaluation criterion
Vendor stability / viability
Implementation track record and references
Support model and SLA
Structured scoring process with weighted criteria for objectivity
Same demonstration script for all vendors

questions.question: What are the main partnership models for IS implementation: staff augmentation, system integration, and managed services? How does the risk and control distribution differ between each model?
questions.model_answer: Staff augmentation: the organisation hires external IS professionals (developers, business analysts, project managers) on a temporary basis to supplement internal teams. The organisation retains full project management control and all delivery risk. The external staff work under internal management direction. Risk: all delivery risk stays with the client; quality depends on internal management capability. Control: maximum — client decides priorities and direction. System integration (SI) partnership: the organisation contracts a specialist SI firm to design, build, and implement the IS. The SI takes responsibility for project delivery according to agreed specifications. Risk is shared: the SI bears delivery risk for specified scope; the client bears requirements completeness risk and business change risk. Control: divided between client (requirements, UAT, acceptance) and SI (delivery methodology, technical decisions). Managed services: the organisation outsources ongoing IS operation (and sometimes management) to a third party. The service provider runs the IS to contracted SLAs. Risk: operational risk transferred to provider; client retains strategic IS risk. Control: minimum — client defines service requirements but not how they are delivered. Choice depends on: internal capability, complexity of IS, strategic importance (core competence IS kept in-house), and risk appetite.
key_points: Staff augmentation: external people, internal control, client bears all delivery risk
System integration: specialist firm delivers, shared risk
Managed services: outsourced operations, provider bears operational risk
Risk distribution clearly stated for each model
Control distribution clearly stated for each model
Factors guiding model choice

questions.question: What are the key elements of an IS service contract and SLA (Service Level Agreement)? What happens when SLAs are not met, and how should SLAs be structured to be meaningful?
questions.model_answer: Key IS service contract elements: scope of service (which systems, which processes, which geographies are covered), SLA metrics, pricing and payment terms, change management process (how additional work is scoped and priced), data ownership and security obligations, liability and indemnification provisions, and termination conditions (notice periods, data return on exit). Key SLA metrics for IS: Availability (e.g. 99.9% uptime during business hours, defined as the percentage of time the system is accessible); Response time (maximum time to acknowledge and begin work on incidents by priority — P1 critical 30 minutes, P2 high 4 hours); Resolution time (maximum time to resolve incidents); Throughput (transaction processing capacity); Change management (time to implement standard changes). When SLAs are not met: most contracts include service credits — automatic financial penalties applied to the next invoice when metrics fall below thresholds. For critical breaches, escalation rights allow the client to escalate to senior provider management, and persistent failure may trigger termination rights. SLA quality: SLAs must be measurable (not vague like 'good service'), monitored with agreed tooling, reported regularly (monthly SLA dashboard), and tied to meaningful financial consequences to create real incentive for the provider.
key_points: Key contract elements: scope, SLA, pricing, change process, data ownership, termination
SLA metrics: availability, response time, resolution time
Priority levels for incident SLAs
Service credits as consequence of SLA failure
SLAs must be measurable and tied to financial consequences
